title: 第 32 節
en: Ksliatriya the bow. Then her brother-in-law, standing in the place of her husband, or a pupil, or an old servant, should make her rise, reciting the verse (Rv. X. 18, 8) :
' Rise, O woman ; come to the world of the living ; thou liest by him whose breath is gone, come back ! Thou hast fulfilled this thy wifehood to him who took thy hand and made thee a mother ^'
'If an old servant (a iSudra) makes her rise, the performer should repeat this verse. He then removes the bow, saying (Rv. X. 18, 9):
' I take the bow from the hand (of the dead), to be to us protection, glory, and strength. Thou art there, we are here with good men ; let us overcome all the wiles of the enemy.'
' If it is a >Sfudra, the same applies as before. After he has stringed the bow, he should walk round the pile, break it, and throw it (on the pile).'

These rules are full of interest, but also of difficulty. What is quite clear is that in Vedic times the widow, though she was placed for a time near her husband on the pile, was not burnt with him. Nothing can be clearer than the words of the verse when she is asked to return to the world of the living. Yet, with all their regard for the divine authority of the Veda, the Brahmans tried to explain it away, and to change the world of the Living into the world of the dead.

That the old servant is not allowed to recite the Vedic verses, shows that these rules date from a time when the Brahmans had secured already the exclusive privilege of the three upper classes, so that no ^ Rajendralal Mitra translates the second half of the verse by : ' become the wife of him who holds thy hand and is willing to marry thee.'
FUNERAL CEREMONIES. 247 Sudrsi was allowed to learn the Veda by heart. The word used, however, in the text is not /Sudra, but Vrishala.
The second half of the verse is not quite clear, but I believe my translation is the right one.

We should observe that the bow given to the dead, if he is a Kshatriya, is broken before it is burnt. It was, therefore, a mere gift, and the idea, that he might have to use it in the next world, seems not to have presented itself to the Indian mind.

Then follow special regulations (IV. 3) as to how the various sacrificial implements should be placed on the different parts of the dead body. These again show a considerable development of the Brahmanic ceremonial, but are otherwise of little interest. That the Vedic Indians with all their devotion to the dead were thrifty, may perhaps be gathered from the fact that the two mill-stones, and all that is made of copper, iron, and earthenware is kept by the son, who is here mentioned for the first time. There may, however, have been another reason, namely, the fact that these things were not combustible.
Then follow rules as to how the animal should be dissected and how again different portions of it should be placed on different parts of the dead body.
' After cutting out the omentum of the Anustara?ii, (the animal that had been brought to the pile) he should cover the head and mouth with it, saying (Rv. X. 16, 7):
cover thyself with their fat and marrow, that the fierce Agni, rejoicing in his glare, the bold one, may not, when he flares up, injure thee.'
'Having taken out the kidneys, he should lay them into his hands, saying (Rv. X. 14, 10) :
' On the good path lain past the two dogs, the brood of Sarama, the four-eyed, the grey ; then go towards the wise Pitns who are happily rejoicing with Yama.'

Here we enter on mythological ground. It is quite clear that the dead is supposed to proceed on a good or right path till he reaches the Pitivs, the fathers, who have died before him. On that path he has to run past two dogs. These dogs are called sarameya, according to the Brahmans_, the sons of Sarama, foureyed, and grey or dark.

Now it is well known that the Greeks also believed that the entrance of the realm of Hades was guarded by a dog, a dog with several heads, called Kerberos by Hesiod. The idea itself is natural enough. Their houses were probably guarded by a dog or dogs, therefore why not the house of the Pit^'^'s ? Still we should remember that this watch-dog has a name in Greece, Kerberos, and that the Greek language supplies no etymology and no meaning of that word. If then it can be proved that sarvari is a name of the night, can there be any possible doubt that the Greek word K€pl3€pos is the same as the Sanskrit yarvara. I hold the original meaning of sarvara to have been dark, like the night, not speckled, and as sarvara has become savara in certain words, I hold that s aba la, the adjective applied to the two dogs of Yama, meant originally dark or grey, like the night.
This identification is so perfectly simple and clear and unobjectionable that it has been accepted by the severest critics ^. But a small acquaintance with ^ Aufrecht, ItuUsche Studien, iv. p. 342.

FUNERAL CEREMONIES. 249 phonetic rules is certainly required in order to see the force of the argument, and we must not be surprised if writers, ignorant of the Sanskrit alphabet and of the invariable correspondence between Sk. palatal s and Greek k, express a learned doubt as to the identity of sarvara and kerberos. Every letter is simply the same in the two words, and if we are to doubt this equation, we may as well doubt the equation of Sk. ma tar and Lat. mater.
Asvalayana then continues, IV. 4 :
' The heart of the animal is placed on the heart of the dead, and two rice-cakes, according to some ; according to others, only when the kidneys are absent.
'Having thus distributed the whole animal, limb by limb, and having covered it with its hide, he recites, while the Prarata water is carried forward, the following verse (X. 16, 8) :
' Agni, do not hurt this cup, which is dear to the gods and to the Somyas (the Fathers). It is that which the gods drink, and in which the immortal gods delight.'
' Bending his left knee he should sacrifice %yaoblations (clarified butter) in the Dakshi??a fire, sayiiig : " Svaha to Agni ! Svaha to Kama (love) ! Svaha to the world ! Svaha to Anumati ! "
' A fifth oblation (is to be poured) on the chest of the deceased, with the verse :
' Thou (Agni) hast verily been born from him, may he (N. N.) now be born from thee. Svaha to the heaven- world ^ ! '

' He now gives the word of command, " Light the fires together ! " If the Ahavaniya-fire reaches him ^ This verse is not in the Rig-veda, and is therefore given in full in the Sutra. See Ykg. Sawih. XXXV. 22 ; Satap.-Brahm. II. 3, 3, 5 ; XII. 5, 2, 15.
first, he should know that it has brought him to the Svarga-world (heaven). He will prosper there, and this, his son, here on earth.
^ If the Garhapatya-fire reaches him first, he should know that it has brought him to the Antarikshaworld (air). He will prosper there, and this, his son, here on earth.
zh: 剎帝利階級的弓。其後，她的姐夫，代替其丈夫的身分站立，或其弟子，或年長的僕人，應使她起身，誦唸該詩句（梨俱吠陀 X. 18, 8）：
'起身吧，女人；來到生者的世界；妳躺在他身邊，他的呼吸已逝，回來吧！妳已為他完成了妻子的天職，他握著妳的手，讓妳成為母親^'
「若一位年長的僕人（一位首陀羅）喚她起身，執行者應誦念此詩句。他隨後移除弓，吟誦道（梨俱吠陀 X. 18, 9）：
「我從死者之手取得弓箭，願它為我們帶來保護、榮耀與力量。汝在彼處，我們與善人在此；願我們克服敵人的一切詭計。」
>如果是首陀羅（Śudra），情況與之前相同。在他張開弓之後，應該繞著柴堆走一圈，打破它，並將它（拋向柴堆）。
這些規則充滿趣味，但也充滿困難。極其明確的是，在吠陀時代，寡婦雖然會被暫時放在丈夫身邊的柴堆旁，卻並未與他一起被焚燒。當她被要求回到生者的世界時，《吠陀》經文的措辭再清楚不過了。然而，儘管婆羅門對《吠陀》的神聖權威懷有敬意，他們仍試圖解釋它，並將生者的世界改變為亡者的世界。
老僕人不被允許誦唸吠陀經文這一事實，表明這些規則源於婆羅門已經確保了三個上層種姓的專有特權的時代，因此沒有 ^ Rajendralal Mitra 將該詩句的後半部分譯為：「成為握著妳的手且願意娶妳之人的妻子。」
喪禮儀式。247 盧陀羅人獲准背誦吠陀。然而，文本中使用的詞並非「盧陀羅」(Sudra)，而是「賤民」(Vrishala)。
這段詩文的後半部並不十分清楚，但我相信我的翻譯是正確的。
應當注意的是，如果死者是剎帝利（Kshatriya），獻給他的弓在焚燒之前會被折斷。因此，這只是一份禮物，而死者在來世可能需要使用它這樣的想法，似乎並未出現在印度人的思想中。
接著是特別規定（IV. 3），涉及如何在死者身體的不同部位放置各種祭祀用具。這些規定同樣顯示了婆羅門祭儀相當程度的發展，但除此之外則少有價值。吠陀印度人雖然對死者恭敬虔誠，但也頗為節儉，這或可從以下事實看出：兩塊磨石以及所有銅製、鐵製和陶製的物品均由兒子保管——此處首次提及兒子。不過，可能另有其他原因，即這些物品本身是不可燃燒的。
隨後則是關於如何解剖該動物的規則，以及如何將其不同部分置於死者身體不同部位的規則。
割下阿努斯塔拉尼（*Anustara?ii*）的網膜後，（即那隻被帶往祭火堆的動物），他應以其覆蓋頭顱和口部，並誦說（《梨俱吠陀》X. 16, 7）：
用它們的脂肪和骨髓覆蓋自己，使得那兇猛的火神（Agni），在他的光芒中歡欣雀躍，這勇敢者，當他熊熊燃燒時，可能不會傷害你。
「取出腎臟後，他應將之放入自己的雙手中，誦說（梨俱吠陀 X. 14, 10）：
「踏上已鋪好的善道，越過莎羅摩（Sarama）的子嗣，那四眼、灰毛的獵犬；之後朝向智慧的毗多（Pitr）們前去，他們與閻摩（Yama）一同歡樂地欣悅著。」
在此我們進入了神話的領域。顯然亡者應當沿著善或正道前行，直到到達**毗多羅斯**（Pitivs），即先於他而亡的諸位先祖。在這條道路上，他必須跑過兩條狗。根據婆羅門人的說法，這些狗被稱為**薩羅梅耶**（sarameya），是薩羅摩的兒子，四眼，毛色灰黑。

現在眾所周知，希臘人也相信冥府哈地斯（Hades）的入口由一隻狗守衛，這隻狗有多個頭顱，赫西俄德（Hesiod）稱之為刻耳柏洛斯（Kerberos）。這個觀念本身再自然不過了。他們的房屋可能由一隻或數隻狗守衛，那麼為什麼地獄之家不也如此呢？然而我們應當記住，這隻看門狗在希臘有一個名字——刻耳柏洛斯——而希臘語卻無法為這個詞提供任何語源學或語義解釋。若夫能夠證明 sarvari 是夜晚的一個名字，那麼希臘詞 Κέρβερος 與梵文 sarvara 係同一詞源，豈能有任何可能的疑慮？我認為 sarvara 的原始含義乃為黑暗的，如同夜晚一般，並非斑紋的；而既然 sarvara 在某些詞中已演變為 savara，我則認為 sabala——即尤摩天（Yama）那兩隻狗身上所用的形容詞——原本的含義亦為黑色的或灰色的，如同夜晚一般。
這種認定是如此完全明白清晰且毫無異議，以至於連最嚴厲的批評者也接受了它^。但只要對^有些許涉獵 ^ Aufrecht, *Italische Studien*, iv. p. 342.
喪禮儀式。249 要看出這個論證的力量，確實需要通曉音韻規則，對於那些不懂梵文字母、不了解梵文顎音 s 與希臘文 k 之間不變對應關係的著述者，若他們對 sarvara 與 kerberos 的同一性表示有學問的懷疑，我們不必感到驚訝。這兩個詞中的每一個字母完全相同，而且如果我們要懷疑這個對等關係，那麼我們也可以同樣懷疑梵文 ma tar 與拉丁文 mater 的對等關係。
阿斯瓦拉亞那（Asvalayana）隨後繼續説道，《梨俱吠陀經》第四卷第四章：
動物的心臟被放在死者的心臟上，根據某些說法，還放上兩個米餅；根據其他說法，則僅在腎臟缺失時才如此。
「既然如此逐肢將整個動物分解開來，並用其皮膚覆蓋之後，他在普拉拉塔聖水被送往前進之際，誦唸下列詩句（X. 16, 8）：
「阿耆尼，勿傷此杯，乃為諸神與頌父（Somyas）所珍愛。此乃諸神所飲之物，不朽之神所歡欣之器。」
他應當屈其左膝，在達剎那火（Dakshiṇa fire）中獻祭酥油（ghee），誦曰：「敬禮阿耆尼（Agni）！敬禮卡瑪（Kama）！敬禮世界！敬禮阿努瑪蒂（Anumati）！」
第五次祭祀（當）倒在亡者的胸口上，伴以下列詩句：
「你（阿格尼）確實從他而生，願他（某某人）現在從你而生。敬禮天界！」
他現在下令說：「一齊點燃火焰！」如果阿哈瓦尼亞火（Ahavaniya-fire）到達他 ^ 本詩句不見於梨俱吠陀（Rig-veda），因此在蘇特拉（Sutra）中全文給出。見耶祖爾吠陀經集（Yjg. Sawih.）XXXV. 22；薩塔帕塔梵書（Satap.-Brahm.）II. 3, 3, 5；XII. 5, 2, 15。
首先，他應當知道它將使他進入斯瓦迦世界（heaven）。他將在那裡昌盛，而他的兒子也將在此世間昌盛。
如果葛哈帕提亞火（Garhapatya-fire）先到達他，他應當知曉它已將他帶往安塔利克沙世界（Antarikshaworld，空氣界）。他將在彼處昌盛，而他的兒子亦將在此地球上昌盛。
